steps.title: Vision Without Action Is a Hallmark Card
steps.body:
You have a mission. You're building a vision. But if nothing changes in your daily life, it's just a nice idea.

This is where the rubber meets the road: what practices will you commit to — daily, weekly, monthly — that turn your vision into reality?

Think of practices as the operating system of your marriage. They run in the background. They don't require motivation or willpower. They're just what you do.

• A daily check-in isn't a 'nice to have.' It's a practice.
• A weekly date night isn't optional. It's a practice.
• A monthly money conversation isn't awkward. It's a practice.

Great marriages aren't built on inspiration. They're built on systems.

Passion fades. Motivation fluctuates.
Systems endure.

Design the practices that will carry your marriage
when motivation is nowhere to be found.

questions.label:
What practices are already working in your marriage?

Maybe you always kiss goodnight. Maybe you have a running joke. Maybe you check in after tough days.

Now: what practices are missing? What do you keep meaning to make consistent but haven't?

Think about the four pillars — Foundation, Connection, Household, Repair. Where do you have systems? Where are you winging it?

steps.title: The Minimum Viable Marriage System
steps.body:
You don't need a complex system. You need a few reliable practices:

Daily (5 minutes):
• A greeting ritual when you reunite
• A check-in: 'What do you need from me today?'

Weekly (30–60 minutes):
• A state-of-the-union check-in — how are we doing this week?
• Time that's just for the two of you, no kids, no phones

Monthly (1–2 hours):
• A money or logistics meeting — proactive, not reactive
• A 'dreaming' conversation — where do we want to go?

Quarterly (half day):
• A bigger check-in — how are the four pillars doing?
• A shared experience — trip, adventure, project

The key isn't doing all of these. The key is picking your non-negotiables and protecting them.

steps.question: The most important daily practice in a marriage is:
options: Talking for 30 minutes about your day
A brief moment of genuine connection — even 60 seconds
feedback: That's great if it works. But for busy couples, even a small moment is powerful.
Yes. A brief but genuine moment of connection — a real hug, a minute of 'how are you actually doing' — is more sustainable and impactful than longer but inconsistent attempts.
Physical intimacy is important, but making it a daily 'should' creates pressure. Focus on daily connection, not daily frequency.
Eating together is great — but are you present or just eating? Connection is about presence, not proximity.

steps.title: From Winging It to System
steps.body:
Before: You intend to check in at dinner about how your day went. But dinner is chaos. Half the time one of you is distracted. You don't have a real conversation.

After (system): You establish a 5-minute ritual. When you get home, you sit on the couch together for exactly five minutes before anyone starts dinner, checks email, or turns on the TV. One rule: no phones. One question: 'What do you need right now?'

Does it solve everything? No. Does it create a reliable moment of connection every single day? Yes.

That's the power of a practice over an intention.

steps.title: Design One Practice
steps.prompt:
Pick ONE practice to commit to for the next 30 days:

• Daily check-in — 5 minutes, same time, no phones
• Weekly sit-down — 30 minutes, one question: 'How are we doing?'
• Monthly money talk — Proactive, not reactive
• Something else: ______________

Write exactly what it is, when you'll do it, and what you'll protect it from.
steps.body:
Pick ONE practice to commit to for the next 30 days:

• Daily check-in — 5 minutes, same time, no phones
• Weekly sit-down — 30 minutes, one question: 'How are we doing?'
• Monthly money talk — Proactive, not reactive
• Something else: ______________

Write exactly what it is, when you'll do it, and what you'll protect it from.

questions.label:
Be honest with yourself: what will get in the way of this practice?

• Being too tired?
• Travel?
• Kids?
• Forgetting?
• Feeling like 'we don't need it' once things are good?

Name the obstacle now, while motivation is high. Then write one sentence about how you'll handle it when it comes.

steps.question: Once a system is established, it will run on autopilot without effort.
feedback: Correct. Even the best systems need maintenance. Life will disrupt them — travel, illness, busy seasons. The key isn't perfection — it's noticing when the system breaks and restarting it. Good systems are easy to restart.
Not quite. All systems need maintenance. The key isn't to build the system that never breaks — it's to build the system that's easy to restart after life interrupts it.

steps.title: Tomorrow: Growing Together — Not Apart
steps.body:
Practices keep you connected in the day-to-day. But what about the long arc of a marriage? How do you make sure you're not just surviving life's seasons — but growing together through them?

Tomorrow: how to keep evolving — as individuals and as a couple.

steps.title: Day Complete
steps.body: Great work today! You're building something real.
